segments.text: The translation model would be speech in English, text in English, and we would generate the output in Japanese.
So it would take both the speech and the text when it was doing translation.
And that would allow it to, number one, basically get a second opinion about whether the transcription was correct.
But also, like, let's say there was some unique information that only appeared in the.
So just to give an example, I read the book, I read the book.
Are both transpired exactly the same way and they're different translations obviously because one is, you know, present or present tense in the other is passing.
So there are examples where adding a cascaded system with those information and a static system would not.
Another thing is iterative refinement. I think this is actually really interesting because large language models have opened up a whole bunch of possibilities for us in this space.
This is like cascading and stacking, but it can be done multiple times and it can be done multiple times with the same model.
So we have an input, we feed it into the bottle, we get an output, and then we feed the output back in and gradually refine it and make it better and better.

And the first time this was done in neural networks was through something called deliberation networks and basically deliberation networks what they do is they take in an output and then they just gradually refine it to make it better and better they used reinforcement learning algorithm to do this where you generated the output and then improved it.

Another thing that's really popular nowadays is diffusion models and I haven't quite decided whether I'll have time to cover diffusion models in depth, but basically the way a diffusion model works is very similar you start out with nothing and then you gradually make it better and better.

The difference between deliberation networks and diffusion models is diffusion models you can train from scratch by basically noisy the input applying noise to the input and training very efficiently and these are very widely used in image generation they're not super widely used in text just because regular auto regressive models are so good for text, but there are a few efforts to do that.
And then a final one is self refine and the idea behind self refine is you actually maybe I can open the paper because paper has a good.
Thank you.
Actually, I thought it had a good figure.

Yeah, so maybe this is a figure so basically what you do is you feed in the input you generate an output and then you ask the model to give you feedback on the output and say yes, this output is good or like let's say you're doing code generation you can say no, this output has an error in it.

And this is a problem with your output and then you feed in both the output and the feedback and ask the model to refine its output and you do this over and over again this allows you to improve the output and this is has ended up being pretty effective and a pretty wide number of tasks.

One caveat about this is your model has to be really good for this to work so only models kind of on the level of GPT for and not on the level of GPT 3.5 have the ability to do this pretty consistently so it is something you need to be aware of.
